symptom: Yellow leaves, especially lower leaves, with soggy or wet soil
likely_cause: Overwatering
treatment: Stop watering immediately. Allow soil to dry completely. Check for root rot by inspecting roots. Repot in fresh dry soil if roots are brown and mushy. Ensure pot has drainage holes.

symptom: Wilting despite moist soil, dark mushy stems at base, yellow drooping leaves, foul smell from pot
likely_cause: Root rot caused by Pythium or Phytophthora fungi from overwatering
treatment: Remove from pot, trim all brown mushy roots, dust with cinnamon or sulfur, repot in fresh well-draining soil, reduce watering significantly going forward.

symptom: Wilting, drooping, dry crispy leaf tips and edges, bone-dry soil, lightweight pot
likely_cause: Underwatering / drought stress
treatment: Water thoroughly until water drains from holes. For severely dehydrated plants, bottom-water in a tray for 30-60 minutes. Establish a more consistent watering schedule.

symptom: Fine webbing between leaves and stems, stippled bronze or silvery leaves, dusty appearance, tiny moving dots
likely_cause: Spider mite infestation; thrives in hot dry conditions
treatment: Isolate plant immediately. Rinse thoroughly under shower or hose. Apply neem oil or insecticidal soap spray, covering undersides of leaves. Increase humidity. Repeat weekly for 3-4 weeks.

symptom: White cottony fuzzy clusters in leaf axils, stems, and undersides of leaves; sticky honeydew residue
likely_cause: Mealybug infestation
treatment: Isolate plant. Dab all visible mealybugs with 70% isopropyl alcohol on cotton swab. Spray with neem oil. Check for new colonies weekly. Severe infestations may require discarding the plant.

symptom: Small dark flies hovering around soil, larvae in topsoil, slow growth and wilting from root damage
likely_cause: Fungus gnats; larvae feed on roots in moist soil
treatment: Allow soil to dry more between waterings (top 2 inches dry). Use yellow sticky traps. Drench soil with 1:4 hydrogen peroxide to water solution. Apply Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis (Bti) for severe cases.

symptom: Brown oval shell-like bumps firmly attached to stems and leaf undersides; sticky honeydew; sooty mold
likely_cause: Scale insect infestation
treatment: Scrape off visible scale with toothpick or soft toothbrush. Wipe with 70% isopropyl alcohol. Apply horticultural oil or neem oil. Treat weekly for 4-6 weeks as eggs hatch.

symptom: White powdery coating on leaves and stems; leaves may yellow and distort; typically starts on upper leaf surface
likely_cause: Powdery mildew fungus; favored by poor air circulation and moderate humidity
treatment: Remove affected leaves. Improve air circulation. Spray with diluted baking soda solution (1 tsp per quart water) or neem oil. Avoid overhead watering. Do not mist affected plants.

symptom: Brown or black circular spots on leaves, often with yellow halo; spots may merge as disease spreads
likely_cause: Fungal leaf spot (Cercospora, Septoria, or Alternaria spp.)
treatment: Remove all affected leaves. Avoid wetting foliage when watering. Improve airflow. Apply copper-based fungicide or neem oil. Avoid overcrowding plants.
affected_plants: especially in high humidity or when leaves stay wet

symptom: Bleached, white, or tan patches on leaves (usually upper surface); crispy texture in affected areas; sharp boundary between healthy and damaged tissue
likely_cause: Sunscorch from direct intense sunlight, especially on plants not adapted to full sun
treatment: Move plant away from direct harsh sunlight. Damaged leaves will not recover but the plant will produce healthy new growth. Transition plants gradually to higher light levels.

symptom: Pale yellow-green leaves uniformly across plant, slow growth, small new leaves, overall washed-out appearance
likely_cause: Nitrogen deficiency; most common nutrient deficiency in houseplants
treatment: Apply balanced liquid fertilizer (high nitrogen like 10-5-5 or 20-20-20) diluted to half strength. Fertilize monthly during growing season. Avoid fertilizing in winter.

symptom: Yellowing between leaf veins while veins remain green (interveinal chlorosis), especially on young leaves
likely_cause: Iron deficiency (often caused by high pH soil preventing iron uptake)
treatment: Apply chelated iron fertilizer. Lower soil pH with acidifying fertilizer or add coffee grounds to soil. Flush soil to remove mineral buildup.

symptom: Brown leaf tips and edges on otherwise healthy plant; usually progresses inward; common in striped or narrow-leaved plants
likely_cause: Fluoride or salt toxicity from tap water or overfertilizing
treatment: Switch to filtered, distilled, or rainwater. Flush soil thoroughly with distilled water to leach accumulated salts. Remove badly affected leaves.

symptom: Clusters of small soft-bodied green, black, white, or pink insects on new growth and leaf undersides; sticky honeydew; distorted curled new leaves
likely_cause: Aphid infestation
treatment: Blast with strong water spray. Apply insecticidal soap or neem oil, coating all surfaces especially undersides. Repeat weekly. Introduce ladybugs outdoors.

symptom: Silver or bronze streaks on leaf surfaces, distorted or scarred new growth, tiny fast-moving insects visible with magnification, black fecal dots
likely_cause: Thrips infestation
treatment: Remove heavily infested leaves. Spray with neem oil, spinosad, or insecticidal soap. Use sticky traps. Isolate from other plants. Repeat every 5-7 days.

symptom: Long stretched spindly stems reaching toward light, wide spacing between leaves, pale color, loss of compact form
likely_cause: Insufficient light / etiolation
treatment: Move plant to brighter location. Prune back leggy stems to encourage bushier growth. For succulents and cacti, direct sun is usually needed. Changes will be visible in new growth.

symptom: Corky, water-soaked, or blistered bumps on leaf undersides or stems; bumps may turn brown or tan
likely_cause: Edema (oedema) — plant takes up water faster than it can transpire; common in overwatered or humid conditions
treatment: Reduce watering frequency. Improve air circulation. Move to brighter location to increase transpiration. Affected areas won't heal but new growth will be normal.

symptom: Darkened water-soaked areas on leaves, limp or mushy tissue, blackening of leaves that doesn't recover, sudden collapse
likely_cause: Frost or cold damage; chilling injury from temperatures too low for tropical plants
treatment: Move plant to warmer location immediately. Remove dead or damaged foliage. Do not overwater cold-stressed plants — roots uptake water poorly. Recovery depends on severity.

symptom: Gray fuzzy mold on leaves, stems, or flowers; affects dying or damaged tissue first; thrives in cool wet conditions
likely_cause: Botrytis cinerea (gray mold) fungus
treatment: Remove all affected plant parts. Improve air circulation. Reduce humidity. Avoid overhead watering. Apply copper-based fungicide. Discard severely affected plants.
affected_plants: most plants in cool humid conditions

symptom: Water-soaked dark lesions on stems or leaves, foul odor, slimy oozing tissue, rapid plant collapse
likely_cause: Bacterial soft rot (Erwinia or Pseudomonas spp.); commonly enters through wounds
treatment: Cut away all affected tissue with sterilized blade. Apply hydrogen peroxide to cut surfaces. Repot in fresh sterile soil. Reduce watering. Severe cases: discard plant to prevent spread.
